question: In the Java client, why does the reactive worker example chain `.thenApply(...)` and `.exceptionally(...)` on `client.newCompleteCommand(job.getKey()).send()` instead of calling `.join()`?

options.text: `.join()` blocks the worker thread until the broker acknowledges completion; chaining callbacks keeps the thread free so jobs can proceed without growing the thread pool.
`.join()` only works inside the Spring Boot Starter; the pure Java client always requires callbacks.
`.join()` skips the broker's retry handling, so a job is never retried after a network blip.
`.thenApply()` is required to deserialize variables into POJOs; `.join()` returns only raw JSON.

a.text: The docs note that `.join()` blocks to wait for the response, holding the worker thread for the network round-trip. Registering callbacks via `.thenApply(...)` / `.exceptionally(...)` keeps the reactive pipeline non-blocking so the framework can overlap many in-flight completions without you sizing a thread pool.

b.text: `.join()` is the same `CompletionStage` API regardless of how the worker is registered; it is available in both the plain Java client and the Spring Boot Starter.

c.text: `.join()` only changes when the calling thread proceeds; retries are a separate concept driven by the broker's job activation logic, not by which terminal operator you use on the future.

d.text: Variable deserialization is independent of `.join()` vs `.thenApply()`; both eventually expose the same response payload through the SDK's models.

explanation: `.join()` is a blocking terminal operator on the SDK's future — it pins the worker thread until the broker acknowledges completion. Chaining `.thenApply(...)` and `.exceptionally(...)` lets the reactive code register callbacks instead, so the thread is released immediately and the framework can keep many job completions in flight without growing a thread pool.
